coredata.dc:title: Active polyvinyl alcohol packaging films incorporated with spermine functionalized carbon dots as antibacterial agents for salmon preservation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Shasha
Li, Jiashu
Ma, Jiale
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: Spermine functionalized carbon dots (Spe-CDs) synthesized by microwave were doped in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to prepare active composite films with antibacterial activity. The Spe-CDs doping could endow the PVA composite film with fluorescence properties, a smoother and denser surface structure, and brownish yellow color. The addition of Spe-CDs also enhanced the performances of the PVA composite films such as the thermal stability, the ultraviolet shielding properties, and the mechanical properties. The three-phase contact angle increased from 52.6° to 84.7° due to the doping of 0.0050 % Spe-CDs, which might be responsible for the decline in water absorption rate and water vapor permeability. The Spe-CDs/PVA composite films displayed certain antioxidant capacity and could completely suppress the reproduction of E. coli and S. aureus with 0.0025 % and 0.0050 % Spe-CDs, respectively. The packing of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films showed good preservation performance on salmon pieces during the storage, such as the decrease in the drip loss, total plate count, TVB-N value and lipid oxidation, and affected the water mobility and distribution. Therefore, the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films displayed great potential for application in food preservation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Active packaging film
Spe-CDs
Antibacterial activity
Salmon preservation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Materials and methods

Preparation of Spe-CDs and the Spe-CDs/PVA film
Identification of Spe-CDs
Fluorescence property and microstructure of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films

Determination of water contact angle, water absorption, water solubility and water vapor permeability (WVP) of the films

Mechanical characteristics of the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films
Preservation performance of the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films on salmon pieces
Statistical Analysis
Results and discussion
Identification of the Spe-CDs
Fluorescent properties and microstructure of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films
XRD, FTIR, XPS and thermogravimetric analysis of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films
Opacity, color change and UV blocking properties of Spe-CDs/PVA films
Water contact angle, water absorption rates and water vapor permeability
Antibacterial and antioxidant performances of Spe-CDs/PVA films
Mechanical properties of Spe-CDs/PVA films
Preservation effect of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films on salmon pieces during storage
Conclusion
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgments

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) Size distribution, (c) Fluorescence lifetime, (d) Fluorescence excitation and emission, and UV absorption spectra of Spe-CDs.

Spe-CDs/PVA composite membrane solution under sunlight (a) and (b) UV light of 365 nm, (c) Visual appearance and (d) Fluorescence images of Spe-CDs/PVA composite membrane, (e) Surface and (f) Cross-sectional SEM images of the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) FTIR spectrum, (c)XPS analysis, (d) Weight retention and (e) First-order derivative curves of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films.
(a) Optical photographs, (b) Opacity, (c) L* , a* and b* values, UV-Vis absorption spectra (d) and transmission spectra (e) of different Spe-CDs /PVA composite films.
(a) Water contact angle, (b) Water absorption rate and solubility, and (c) Water vapor permeability of different Spe-CDs/PVA films.

(a) Typical photographs of E.coli and S.aureus plates with the treatment of different Spe-CDs/PVA composite film solutions and the corresponding (b) survival rates, (c) ABTS scavenging rates and (d) DPPH scavenging rates of the different Spe-CDs/PVA composite films.
(a) Thickness, (b) TS, (c) EB and (d) EM for Spe-CDs/PVA composite films.
(a) Photographs, (b) Drip loss, (c) TVC, (d) TVN-N and (e) TBARS of the salmon pieces during the storage of 4 °C treated by the different films.

The T 2 relaxation curves of salmon pieces during storage with the treatment of (a) PE film, (b) PVA film and (c) 0.0050 % Spe-CDs/PVA composite film, (d) the corresponding relaxation time T 22 of the immobilized warer.
(a) T 2 weighted pseudo-color images of salmon pieces with the treatment of PE film, PVA film and 0.0050 % Spe-CDs/PVA composite film and (b) the quantitative signal intensity during the storage.
Comparison between the composition films doped with CDs in this study and other works.

row.entry: 104.8 CFU/mL (after 5 days at 4 °C, 0.5 % CDs, fried meatballs)

head.ce:title: Active polyvinyl alcohol packaging films incorporated with spermine functionalized carbon dots as antibacterial agents for salmon preservation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Marine Food Processing and Safety Control, Dalian Polytechnic University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Spermine functionalized carbon dots (Spe-CDs) synthesized by microwave were doped in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to prepare active composite films with antibacterial activity. The Spe-CDs doping could endow the PVA composite film with fluorescence properties, a smoother and denser surface structure, and brownish yellow color. The addition of Spe-CDs also enhanced the performances of the PVA composite films such as the thermal stability, the ultraviolet shielding properties, and the mechanical properties. The three-phase contact angle increased from 52.6° to 84.7° due to the doping of 0.0050 % Spe-CDs, which might be responsible for the decline in water absorption rate and water vapor permeability. The Spe-CDs/PVA composite films displayed certain antioxidant capacity and could completely suppress the reproduction of E. coli and S. aureus with 0.0025 % and 0.0050 % Spe-CDs, respectively. The packing of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films showed good preservation performance on salmon pieces during the storage, such as the decrease in the drip loss, total plate count, TVB-N value and lipid oxidation, and affected the water mobility and distribution. Therefore, the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films displayed great potential for application in food preservation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Salmon is usually considered a popular food due to its abundant protein content, polyunsaturated fatty acids, astaxanthin and minerals as well as its unique taste and flavor (Colombo & Mazal, 2020; Hixson, 2014; Faizan et al., 2013). However, salmon is prone to spoilage after harvest, with a short shelf-life primarily caused by microbiological, enzymatic reactions as well as autolytic degradation. The spoilage of the salmon leads to the degradation of protein and produces off-odor compounds such as volatile ammonia, organic acids, sulfur-containing compounds and aldehydes, which impact the sensory properties and contribute to the quality degradation of salmon. Thus, the suppression of the spoilage microorganisms’ growth during storage and distribution has great practical significance in prolonging the shelf life of food based on the economic and technical points.

Nowadays, several synthetic and natural antibacterial agents have been developed for food preservation, such as vanillin, plant polyphenols, essential oils, lysozymes, metal nanoparticles, metal oxide nanoparticles and carbon dots (Fahmy et al., 2020; Ahmed et al., 2020; Van Haute et al., 2017; Soro et al., 2021). Among these, carbon dots (CDs) with antimicrobial activity attract widespread attention because of their advantages such as particle size adjustability, simple and inexpensive synthesis methods, good biocompatibility, and ease of functionalization, and offer new possibilities for the inhibition of microorganism growth in food during processing and transport (Lin et al., 2022). The size, surface charge and functional groups play pivotal roles in relating to the antibacterial activity of CDs. Generally, the negatively charged bacteria are more likely to adsorb the CDs with positive charges through electrostatic interactions, which promotes the internalization of CDs in bacteria cells. In addition, the internalized CDs can destroy the integrity of bacterial cells and cause the leakage of cytoplasmic material, and the reactive oxygen species induced by CDs can destroy DNA, protein and cell metabolism. The antimicrobial activity of CDs can be enhanced through surface functional group modification such as amine and quaternary ammonium groups.

In recent years, composite active packaging films doped with antibacterial CDs gradually attracted people's attention, and the addition of CDs enhanced the antibacterial performance of the films and also provides distinct features such as UV shielding, fluorescence, antioxidant as well as better mechanical properties (Zhao et al., 2022b). Nowadays, CDs derived from different sources have been incorporated with different film matrixes including chitosan, cellulose nanofiber, polyvinyl alcohol and gelatin, and the active composite films could prolong the shelf-life of multiple types of food such as fruit and vegetable, meat and aquatic products (Moradi et al., 2021). CDs synthesized from banana paste through hydrothermal reaction were added to polyvinyl alcohol matrix for the preparation of composite active packaging films, and the CDs endowed the excellent antibacterial and antioxidant activity of the active film, and the active films with 0.50 % CDs could effectively achieve the preservation of fried meatballs, banana and jujube (Zhao et al., 2022a). Khan et al. developed composite packaging films incorporating with N, P doped CDs using chitosan/starch as film matrix for meat preservation, and the strong antimicrobial activity of the composite films effectively reduced the bacterial growth of the wrapped meat (Khan et al., 2023). Guo et al. (2024) synthesized CDs with antibacterial activity derived from Sophora Japonica extract by hydrothermal method, and the doping of CDs improved the UV shielding and the mechanical properties of the gelatin-based film and the coating of the composite film on the strawberries could inhibit the bacteria growth and prolong the storage period of the strawberries. Fan et al. (2020; 2021) incorporated the CDs derived from kelp into the chitosan-based coating, which could enhance the antibacterial and freshness-keeping effect of the film on fresh-cut cucumber coupled with modified atmosphere packaging and ultrasound treatment. Rhim’s group found that the addition of N-functionalized CDs and Brassica oleracea biowaste derived CDs could enhance the UV-blocking properties, antioxidant activity and high antimicrobial activity of the cellulose nanofiber-based film, and the active packaging could be applied to the preservation of fruit, pork meat and aquatic products (Ezati et al., 2022; Wagh et al., 2023; Tammina & Rhim, 2023; Sul et al., 2024).

Spermine functionalized CDs (Spe-CDs) synthesized by microwave heating have been proved to have the best antibacterial activity due to their nanoscale size, high surface positive charge and high reactive oxygen species (ROS) production in our previous publication (Cheng et al., 2023). The goal of this paper was to develop novel active composite films by incorporating these highly antibacterial Spe-CDs into the biodegradable polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix for the preservation of salmon meat. The influence of Spe-CDs incorporation on the properties of composite films such as fluorescence characteristics, morphology, mechanical properties, water vapor permeability, UV shielding performance, antioxidant and antimicrobial properties was explored systematically. Finally, the preservation performance of the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films on the salmon pieces was evaluated by the measurement of drip loss, total viable count (TVC)), total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N), lipid oxidation as well as the water mobility and distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: The precursors including glucose, sodium chloride and spermine (Spe) for the synthesis of Spe-CDs were all analytical grade, among which the Spe was purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), and the glucose and sodium chloride were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). PVA powder with 98 −99 % alcoholysis degree (1799 type) purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) was used as the film matrix. Tryptic soy agar (TSA) and tryptic soy broth (TSB) were purchased from Hope Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Qingdao, China). Salmon meat was purchased from a local market in Dalian, China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Spe-CDs and the Spe-CDs/PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: Spe-CDs were prepared using glucose and spermine as precursors by microwave heating at 700 W for 5 minutes referring to the steps described in our previous publication (Cheng et al., 2023). The lyophilized Spe-CDs were stored at −20 °C for further experiments. PVA films doped with antibacterial Spe-CDs were prepared on the basis of Alas’s method (Alas et al., 2022) with some modifications. Firstly, the film matrix solution was prepared by the dispersing of 1.5 g PVA power in 19 mL deionized water, which was heated for 30 min at 95 °C with magnetic stirring to obtain a transparent solution. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was added by 0.3 g glycerol as a plasticizer. Then, different masses of the Spe-CDs were dissolved in the solution with various concentrations of 0.0000 %, 0.0025 %, 0.005 %, 0.0075 % and 0.0100 %. Eight milliliters of the Spe-CDs/PVA solution was decanted into a plastic petri plate with a diameter of 90 mm, which was placed at room temperature for 2 days for the evaporation of water to form the film. Based on the Spe-CDs concentration, the films were labeled as 0.0000 %, 0.0025 %, 0.0050 %, 0.0075 % and 0.0100 % Spe-CDs/PVA, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of Spe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ten microliters of 1 mg/mL Spe-CDs solution were deposited on a carbon net, which was transferred to a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) for the morphology observation of Spe-CDs after the water evaporation at room temperature. The distribution of Spe-CDs particle size was determined by conducting statistical analysis of the obtained TEM images using ImageJ software. The fluorescence lifetime of Spe-CDs was obtained using an FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Co., Ltd., Livingston, UK). The UV-Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Spe-CDs were also characterized using a Lambda 35 UV-Vis spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) and a Hitachi F-2700 spectrometer to identify the successful synthesis of Spe-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence property and microstructure of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence images of the composite films were captured using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Ti-s, Nikon, Japan) at the excitation wavelengths of 361–387 nm. A SU8010 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) was applied to view the surface and cross-sectional microstructure of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films after freezing cutting treatment using liquid nitrogen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray diffraction (XRD)
ce:section.ce:para: Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and thermogravimetric analysis of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films

The crystal structures of the Spe-CDs and the composite films were characterized by an XRD-7000S X-ray diffraction (Shimadzu, Japan) in the range from 10 to 70° at 5°/min. FTIR spectra of the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films were captured using a Frontier FTIR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) in the scanning range of 400–4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1 utilizing 32 scan rates. Elemental analysis of Spe-CDs and the composite films was carried out by a K-Alpha XPS analyzer (Thermo Fisher, United States). The thermogravimetric analysis curves of the films were tested using a thermogravimetric analysis instrument (TGA550, Waters, USA) in the temperature range from 30 to 650 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min to evaluate the thermal stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of opacity, color and UV-shielding characteristics of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Films were cut into pieces with the size of 10 mm× 40 mm, which were inserted into a quartz cuvette. The UV-Vis absorbance of the film was determined by the Lambda 35 UV-Vis spectrometer with a wavelength of 600 nm for 5 times, and the opacity values were calculated as the ratios of the absorbance to the thicknesses of films. The color of the film pieces was measured by an UltraScan PRO colorimeter (Hunter Associates Lab., Inc., USA.) equipped with a D65 light source, and the results were expressed in L*a* b* system. The UV-Vis absorption and transmission spectra were also scanned from 200 to 600 nm by the same UV-Vis spectrometer to assess the UV-shielding characteristics of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of water contact angle, water absorption, water solubility and water vapor permeability (WVP) of the films
ce:section.ce:para: A contact angle measuring instrument (DSA25, KRÜSS, Germany) was used to determine the water contact angle of the films. The angle produced by the initial contact between the water droplet and the film was recorded, and the corresponding image was saved.

For water absorption determination, the film was firstly cut into 3 cm × 3 cm pieces, which were dried for 24 h at 105 °C. The dried films were weighed and recorded as the initial weight M0, and soaked in 30 mL of distilled water for 24 h. Then, some pieces of the films were gently wiped off the surface water and weighed as the weight Me for the calculation of water absorption rate, which was expressed as (Me- M0)/M0× 100 %. The other pieces of the films were dried for 24 h at 105 °C again, and the dried film pieces were weighted and recorded as the weight Mf for the calculation of water solubility according to the equation WS= (Mf- M0)/M0× 100 %.

About 50 g of anhydrous calcium chloride was placed in a conical flask, which was then dried for 24 hours at 105 ℃. The flask was sealed with film samples and weighed. Then, the sealed flasks were kept under 75 % relative humidity and 25 ℃ conditions for 10 h, and the weight of the flasks were weighed after 3 h and weighed every one hour during the left 7 h. The WVP values were calculated according to the formula: WVP = (ΔW*d)/(Δt * A*ΔP), in which ΔW is the mass change of the weighed flask (g) during the time Δt (s), d is the mean value of film thickness (m), A is the effective area of the film (m2), and Δp is the partial water vapor pressure difference across the film (Pa).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial and antioxidant activities of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activities of Spe-CDs/PVA films on S. aureus and E. coli were estimated through the plate count technique according to the reference (Hu et al., 2022). Firstly, the UV-sterilized film solution was mixed at a ratio of 1:1 with the bacterial suspension (105 CFU/mL). After shaking for 2 h at 37 °C, 100 μL of the mixture was spread evenly on an agar plate, which was incubated under the temperature of 37 °C for 12 h and the number of surviving bacterial colonies was counted and recorded. The survival rate was defined as the ratio of the number of surviving bacterial colonies recorded in the treatment group to that in the control group.

The antioxidant capacities of the Spe-CDs/PVA films were estimated through DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activities. Firstly, 0.1 g Spe-CDs/PVA composite films was mixed with 4 mL of newly prepared ethanolic DPPH solution or ABTS assay solution. After 30 min in the darkness for reaction, the absorbance of the DPPH/ABTS solution (A0) and the mixture solutions with film samples (Ai) was recorded at 517 nm and 734 nm, respectively. The DPPH/ABTS scavenging rate of the film samples were calculated as DPPH/ABTS scavenging rate = (1- Ai/ A0) * 100 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical characteristics of the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Randomly selecting six positions of a film were used to determine the thickness of the film by a micrometer (accurate to 0.001 mm). Elongation at break (EB), tensile strength (TS) and elastic modulus (EM) of the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films were analyzed and collected from the output data of a TA-XT plus texture analyzer (Stable Micro systems, London, UK), and each sample was measured six times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preservation performance of the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films on salmon pieces
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh salmon flesh was divided into pieces with a mass of about 10 g, and each salmon piece was placed in a plastic cup with a bottom diameter of 5 cm, a top diameter of 7 cm, and a height of 7 cm, which was sealed with PE, pure PVA or Spe-CDs/PVA composite films through pulling and pressing. The thicknesses of the PE, pure PVA and Spe-CDs/PVA composite films were about 0.017, 0.143 and 0.149 mm, respectively. The salmon pieces did not contact with the films. The cups were placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C and were randomly sampled for the testing of physicochemical parameters every two days. The calculation of mass loss percentages of salmon pieces was carried out according to the difference between the initial and final weights during storage. The TVB-N values of salmon samples were determined using Conway’s micro-diffusion method described in our previous research in detail (Lin et al.). The TVC values were measured referring to the previously reported method (Chen et al., 2023) with a little modification. About 5 g of salmon meat was homogenized for 1 min after mixing with 45 mL of the sterilized 0.9 % NaCl solution. Then, the homogenized sample was diluted by 10 times using 0.9 % NaCl solution, and 100 μL of the diluted solution was dispersed on an agar plate and then cultured at 30 °C for 48 h. The values of thiobarbituric acid-reactive substances (TBARS) were determined to estimate the degree of salmon lipid oxidation. Firstly, 2 g of salmon meat was homogenized after mixing with 10 mL of trichloroacetic acid solution (7.5 % m/v), which was centrifuged for 10 minutes at 8000 g. Then, 2 mL of the supernatant was mixed with 2 mL of 0.02 mol/L thiobarbituric acid solution, which was heated for 40 min in a boiling water bath. The absorbance of the obtained solution after cooling to room temperature was determined at 532 nm, and the TBARS values were calculated based on the following formula: TBARS (mg/kg) = A532 nm* 2.77. T 2 transverse relaxation data and T 2 weighted imaging of the salmon pieces were measured using a MesoQMR23–060H NMR analyzer (Suzhou Niumag Analytical Instrument Co., Suzhou, China) equipped with a permanent magnet of 0.5 T according to the method reported in Lin’s publication (Lin et al., 2021). Before the measurement, the surface water of the sample was absorbed using filter papers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical Analysis
ce:section.ce:para: SPSS 19.0 software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) was applied to analyze the significance of the data using one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s test. All the figures in this paper were plotted based on the obtained data by Origin 2021 software (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Identification of the Spe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, Spe-CDs were characterized to verify the successful preparation of CDs similar to our previous research (Cheng et al., 2023). Fig. 1(a) and 1(b) show the TEM image of Spe-CDs and the corresponding plot of size distribution. The synthesized Spe-CDs showed a roughly spherical shape and good dispersion with an average diameter of 3.25 ± 0.73 nm, which was consistent with our previous research (Cheng et al., 2023). The fluorescence lifetime of the Spe-CDs in Fig. 1(c) was about 3.43 ns. Fig. 1(d) displays the UV absorption spectra of Spe-CDs and the excitation and emission fluorescence spectra. The UV absorption spectrum displayed broad absorption bands within the 260–360 nm range, in which the absorption peak around 290 nm and the shoulder peak around 330 nm were attributed to π→π* transition of conjugated CC bonds and n→π* transition of the CO and CN bonds (Zhou et al., 2007). The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of Spe-CDs were at 427 and 503 nm due to the existence of sp2 hybrid carbon atom.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent properties and microstructure of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent properties of PVA solutions with different concentrations of Spe-CDs and the corresponding Spe-CDs/PVA composite films are shown in Fig. 2(a)-(d). Figs. 2(a) and 2(b) show the photos of the PVA solution doped with Spe-CDs under sunlight and UV light of 365 nm. The color of the PVA solution with 0.0000 % Spe-CDs was transparent, which gradually turned to light brown when the concentration of Spe-CDs increased from 0.0025 % to 0.0100 %. The fluorescence of the film solution also increased with the addition of Spe-CDs due to the excellent fluorescence characteristics of carbon dots. The color of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films in Fig. 2(c) under sunlight also gradually turned to light brown as the doping of Spe-CDs similar to the Spe-CDs/PVA film solution. Under UV irradiation conditions, in Fig. 2(d), there was no fluorescence intensity observed in the pure PVA membrane, while the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films exhibited varying degrees of blue fluorescence as the increase of Spe-CDs concentration. The SEM images of the surface and cross-sectional Spe-CDs/PVA films are shown in Fig. 2(e) and (f) to assess the impact of Spe-CDs doping on the microstructure of PVA films. The surface of pure PVA film was smooth with some wavy clumps, and the doping of Spe-CDs improved the surface smoothness of the PVA film accompanied by the disappearance of wavy clumps, suggesting the good compatibility between Spe-CDs and the PVA matrix. A similar phenomenon was also reported by Wagh et al. (2023) in the research about the incorporation of CDs from Brassica oleracea into cellulose nanofiber-based films. The cross-sectional microstructure of the PVA films was not influenced by the doping of Spe-CDs as shown in Fig. 2(f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD, FTIR, XPS and thermogravimetric analysis of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD spectrum of Spe-CDs and Spe-CDs/PVA composite films in Fig. 3(a) demonstrated that Spe-CDs possessed a wide diffraction peak with 2θ value of 21°, while the pure PVA film displayed a diffraction peak with 2θ value of 20.2°. With the increase of Spe-CDs doping concentration, intensities of the diffraction peak with 2θ value of 21° increased, which revealed that the incorporation of PVA did not influence the amorphous structure of Spe-CDs.

The FTIR spectra of Spe-CDs and Spe-CDs/PVA composite films depicted in Fig. 3(b) were utilized to analyze the functional groups on the Spe-CDs surface and their interactions with the PVA film matrix at various concentrations. The peaks within the range of 3400–3200 cm−1 and 1650–1330 cm−1 of Spe-CDs corresponded to the stretching vibration and the bending vibration of N-H, which demonstrated the functionalization of amino groups from spermine on the surface of CDs. However, the differences between the absorption spectra of the pure PVA film and Spe-CDs/PVA composite films with 0.00 %, 0.0025 %, 0.0050 %, 0.0075 % and 0.0100 % Spe-CDs could be ignored. This phenomenon could be explained by the low concentration of Spe-CDs in PVA films, and the characteristic absorption peaks of Spe-CDs were masked by the peaks of PVA.

The XPS results in Fig. 3(c) observed three peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s in Spe-CDs, and two peaks corresponding to C1s and O1s in PVA matrix. However, the XPS spectra of composite films with 0.0025 %, 0.0050 %, 0.0075 % and 0.0100 % Spe-CDs were similar to that of the PVA matrix, and the characteristic N1s peak of Spe-CDs was not observed in the Spe-CDs doped composite films. These results might be due to the low doping amount of Spe-CDs in films with a highest amount of 0.0100 %.

Fig. 3(d) illustrates the weight changes of the composite films across various Spe-CDs concentrations, while Fig. 3(e) provides an overview of the corresponding first-order derivative results. All Spe-CDs/PVA composite films revealed multiple steps of weight loss, and the initial thermal degradation stage took place within a range of 30–150 °C, attributed to the loss of water, glycerol, and some compounds with low molecular weight. The second thermal degradation stage was observed between 150 and 400 °C, resulting from the thermal degradation of PVA side chains and main chain, which was predominant decomposition stage of PVA-based films. Notably, within this range, the curve for the Spe-CD/PVA film shifted upwards as the concentration of Spe-CD increased. The weight loss of pure PVA film at 350 °C was up to 75 %, while that of 0.0100 % Spe-CDs/PVA composite film was about 55 %. This result suggested that the addition of Spe-CDs could partly improve the thermal characteristics of PVA films. The third thermal degradation stage occurred in the temperature range of 400–500 °C, which might be ascribed to the degradation of the residues after the second thermal degradation of PVA-based films, and the differences between the weight losses of pure PVA film and Spe-CDs/PVA composite films might be due to the degradation of CDs structure (Yang et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2022a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Opacity, color change and UV blocking properties of Spe-CDs/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4(a) displays the optical pictures of the different Spe-CDs/PVA composite films, which can visualize the opacity of each film. The PVA film without CDs doping was colorless and transparent, and the addition of Spe-CDs endowed the PVA films with different degrees of brownish yellow as the concentration of the Spe-CDs increased. However, the composite films still displayed good opacity even when the addition of Spe-CDs was as high as 0.0100 %. The opacity of the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films in Fig. 4(b) significantly increased when the concentration of Spe-CDs increased to 0.0075 % and 0.0100 %. Fig. 4(c) displays the color changes of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films including L* , a* and b* values. The Spe-CDs addition exerted minimal influence on the lightness of the films, but led to the increase of b* values, which were consistent with the brownish yellow of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films in Fig. 4(a). The growth in the negative a* values indicated the increase in the greenness of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films. Figs. 4(d) and 4(e) present the UV-Vis absorption and transmission spectra of the composite films doped with Spe-CDs within 200–600 nm, serving to assess their light-shielding capabilities. It is worth noting that the absorbances of the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films in the 200–400 nm range were notably higher than that of the pure PVA film, and the absorbances of the Spe-CD/PVA composite films around 270 nm increased with the introduction of Spe-CDs, owing to their ultraviolet absorption. In Fig. 4(e), the pure PVA film displayed maximal ultraviolet transmittance across the entire scanning range of 200–600 nm. However, the incorporation of Spe-CDs diminished the transmittance within the 200–400 nm range. Therefore, the doping of Spe-CDs mainly improved the UV blocking capacity in the region of 200–400 nm for the PVA matrix film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle, water absorption rates and water vapor permeability
ce:section.ce:para: The water contact angle (θ) is a critical indicator to characterize the surface wettability of the packing film. In Fig. 5(a), the θ value of 52.6° for the pure PVA film indicated that it was more hydrophilic. Then, the θ values of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films increased to 67.5°, 73.6°, 82.4° and 84.7° when the addition of Spe-CDs increased from 0.0025 % to 0.0100 %, which suggested that the presence of Spe-CDs caused the decrease of hydrophilicity of PVA film. This phenomenon might be illustrated by the interaction between the PVA matrix and the surface groups of Spe-CDs, which hindered the wetting of water on the film at the initial contact. Mao et al. (2023) also reported that the θ value of alginate matrix films increased with the addition of nitrogen-functionalized CDs when the nitrogen-functionalized CDs concentration was less than 1 %. Fig. 5(b) shows the variations in water absorption rates and water solubility of PVA-based films as the addition of Spe-CDs. The water absorption rates significantly decreased with the increase of Spe-CDs concentrations, and the water solubility decreased at higher Spe-CDs concentrations. This phenomenon demonstrated the enhanced water stability of the film due to the doping of Spe-CDs and was perhaps attributed to the reduction in surface hydrophilicity reflected by the water contact angle. Water vapor permeability is very important for packaging materials to prevent the water loss of food during storage. The water vapor permeability of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films in Fig. 5(c) slightly decreased with the addition of Spe-CDs, which suggested that the doping of Spe-CDs enhanced the permeation resistance of water vapor going through the PVA matrix films. Wen et al. (2023) reported that the turmeric CDs addition could decrease the permeability of water vapor through the chitosan matrix films, which was ascribed to the more compact and orderly structure and the increase in film thickness induced by the CDs addition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial and antioxidant performances of Spe-CDs/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: Antibacterial and antioxidant performances were critical for the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films as active packaging and evaluated in this section. Fig. 6(a) and 6(b) show the colony growth of E. coli and S. aureus handled by the PVA film solutions with different concentrations of Spe-CDs and the calculated survival rates. The pure PVA film displayed no inhibitory action on the growth of bacteria compared to the control group, but promoted the reproduction of bacteria due to its good biodegradability (Xu et al., 2009). However, the bacterial colony of E. coli and S. aureus decreased as the increment of Spe-CDs doping, and only 0.0050 % Spe-CDs in PVA film could comprehensively restrain the growth of two types of microorganisms. The antibacterial property of the composite film was predominantly linked with the antibacterial activity of Spe-CDs due to its small size and positive surface charge. The initial electrostatic interactions between the positively charged Spe-CDs and negatively charged bacteria promoted the internalization of CDs and the bacterial apoptosis, which could induce physical damage to the cell wall and membrane of bacteria with the leakage of cytoplasmic material, and the oxidative stress of bacteria with DNA and protein damage (Cheng et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2023). In addition, the antibacterial performances of the composite films were directly dependent on the concentration of Spe-CDs, and the higher Spe-CDs concentration led to a more significant inhibitory effect on the two kinds of microorganisms. Zhu et al. (2019) synthesized N-doped CDs polyurethane composite films with negative charges and studied their inhibitory effects on iron bacteria, E. coli and S. aureus, and observed that the antibacterial activity of the composite film was dependent on the N-doped CDs concentration, and increased with the increase of N-doped CDs concentration. Figs. 6(c) and 6(d) show the differences in antioxidant activity of the different composite films with 0.00 %, 0.0025 %, 0.0050 %, 0.0075 % and 0.0100 % Spe-CDs in the PVA matrix based on the ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activities. It was noted that there was no antioxidant activity for the pure PVA film, but the antioxidant activities including ABTS and DPPH values of the Spe-CDs/PVA film were gradually enhanced by the doping of Spe-CDs. Similar phenomena have been reported by the previous publication, which were ascribed to the appearance of abundant functional groups (such as -OH, -COOH, and so on) on the CDs surface that participating in free radical scavenging through hydrogen donating behavior (Zhao et al., 2022a; Guo et al., 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of Spe-CDs/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical properties are important indicators to assess the performance of the active packaging films (Ezati & Rhim, 2021). The changes in the mechanical properties including thickness, TS, EB, and EM of the Spe-CDs/PVA films are displayed in Fig. 7. The pure PVA film exhibited a minimum thickness of around 0.14 ± 0.009 mm, and the thicknesses of Spe-CDs/PVA films with 0.0025 %, 0.0050 %, and 0.0075 % CDs were not significantly different from that of the pure PVA membrane (p > 0.05). However, the thickness of the composite film with 0.0100 % Spe-CDs significantly increased to 0.19 ± 0.031 mm (p < 0.05). In addition, the doping of Spe-CDs caused the significant increase in the TS values of the PVA films (p < 0.05) in Fig. 7(b), which might be ascribed to the interaction between the CDs and the PVA molecules as well as the change in the microstructure (Roy & Rhim, 2020). Meanwhile, there was no significant influence on the EB and EM values of the composite films with the addition of Spe-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preservation effect of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films on salmon pieces during storage
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films on the freshness of salmon pieces during the storage of 4 °C compared with the PE film and the pure PVA film. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the fresh salmon have a distinctive orange-red color and clear patterns, and the appearance quality of salmon pieces with the three different treatments showed varying degrees of decline with the increase of the storage time. The surface color of the salmon pieces treated by PE and PVA films became lighter after 8 days of storage, which was accompanied by oil exudation. However, the color change of salmon samples treated with 0.0050 % Spe-CDs/PVA composite film was not significant, and the fat patterns were still clear. Drip loss, TVC, TVB-N and TBARS values are important traditional indicators to evaluate the freshness of aquatic products (Hassoun & Karoui, 2017). The drip losses of salmon pieces as shown in Fig. 8(b) all increased during the storage. Among them, the drip losses of the samples treated by the PE film and the pure PVA film increased to 24.18 ± 7.56 % and 28.78 ± 3.72 %, respectively, while that treated by the 0.0050 % Spe-CDs/PVA composite film raised to 20.12 ± 2.21 %. The lowest drip loss of the group treated by the 0.0050 % Spe-CDs/PVA composite film was ascribed to the excellent antimicrobial activity of Spe-CDs. The variations of TVC, TVB-N and TBARS values of salmon pieces in Fig. 8(b)-(d) displayed similar increase trends to the drip loss during storage. Therefore, compared to the commercial PE film, the pure PVA film could slightly promote the increase of microorganisms in salmon pieces during the storage, which led to the most serious spoilage of salmon as well as the highest values of drip loss and TVB-N. The doping of Spe-CDs in the PVA matrix could effectively suppress the microbial growth in salmon pieces, and the antioxidant activity of the 0.0050 % Spe-CDs/PVA composite film resulted in the lowest TBARS value. Ezati et al. developed nanocomposite films using chitosan/starch matrix incorporated with green-tea-derived NP-CDs, which could restrain the bacteria growth and decrease the color change in ground meat (Khan et al., 2023). The comparison between the composite films doped with CDs in this study and other works are summarized in Table 1. The lower doping amount of Spe-CDs in film matrix in this study obtained equivalent microbial inhibition effect.

Besides, the impact of Spe-CDs/PVA composite film on the changes in the water freedom and distribution of salmon pieces during the process of 4 °C storage was also explored using LF NMR and MRI. Fig. 9(a), 9(b) and 9(c) show the T 2 relaxation curves of salmon pieces during storage treated by the PE, PVA and 0.0050 % Spe-CDs/PVA composite films, respectively. There were mainly three relaxation peaks with a relaxation time of less than 10 ms (T 21), 10–100 ms (T 22), and 100–1000 ms (T 23) in the salmon pieces, which were classified as bound, immobilized and free water, respectively. With the increasing storage time, the immobilized water peak gradually moved towards a shorter relaxation time, and the peak intensity declined, which reflected the decrease in the mobility and the content of immobilized water in salmon during the storage. The changes in the relaxation time of dominant immobilized water were summarized in Fig. 9(d), and the samples treated by the Spe-CDs/PVA composite film showed the minimum reduction in the mobility of immobilized water. The diminution in T 22 might be related to the protein degradation induced by the spoilage of salmon meat during storage.

Fig. 10 (a) and 10(b) show the T 2 weighted pseudo-color images of salmon pieces and the quantitative signal intensity during the storage. In the pseudo-color images, the red and blue colors represent the high and low proton density, respectively. The fat lines in salmon pieces showed high signal intensity. During the storage, the MRI intensity of the salmon pieces was weakened under the three treatments, suggesting a decrease in the moisture content of salmon meat during the storage process. The quantitative signal intensities of T 2 weighted images showed a significant decrease with the storage time, and the decrease trend was consistent with the drip loss.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In this research, active packing films based on a PVA matrix with antibacterial activity were prepared with the doping of Spe-CDs synthesized by microwave heating, and the influence of Spe-CDs doping on the film performance was explored. The Spe-CDs/PVA composite films displayed blue fluorescence, smoother surface microstructure, and brownish-yellow color. The doping of Spe-CDs improved the thermal stability, mechanical properties, and water resistance characteristics of the PVA matrix films and endowed the composite films with ultraviolet shielding features. These characteristics exhibited concentration dependence on the doped Spe-CDs within the range of 0.0000 %-0.0100 %. The excellent antibacterial activity and antioxidant capacity of Spe-CDs/PVA composite films decreased the drip loss, TOC, TVB-N values, and lipid oxidation of salmon pieces during the 4 °C storage process, and affected the water mobility and distribution. The results have demonstrated the potential application of the Spe-CDs/PVA composite films as an active packaging material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Mingqian Tan: Writing – review & editing. Shasha Cheng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Jiashu Li: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jiale Ma: Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there was no known competing financial interests or personal relationships to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Key Research Project of Educational Department of Liaoning Province (JYTZD2023022).
